llms.txt

Template Cache

Outpost uses a two-level caching system to keep pages fast. Liquid templates are compiled to PHP once and cached on disk. Rendered HTML output is cached separately with a 1-hour TTL, so the database is queried only once per page per hour — regardless of traffic.

Two-level caching overview

When a visitor requests a page, Outpost works through two distinct caching layers before querying the database or executing template logic:

LayerWhat is cachedLocationTTL
Template cache Compiled PHP version of each Liquid .html template cache/templates/ Until the source file changes (mtime-based)
HTML page cache Full rendered HTML output for each URL cache/html/ 1 hour (3600 seconds)

On a cache hit for the HTML layer, Outpost reads a file from disk and sends it to the browser. No template compilation, no database queries, no PHP function calls. This is the most impactful performance optimization in the system.

Template cache

Liquid templates (.html files in your theme directory) are not executed directly. Outpost's template engine compiles them to plain PHP files the first time they are requested. Each subsequent request runs the compiled PHP file directly, bypassing the compilation step entirely.

How compilation works

The compiler reads your .html template, parses the Liquid-like syntax, and converts each tag to its PHP equivalent:

Template tagCompiled PHP
{{ field_name }}<?= cms_text('field_name') ?>
{{ field_name | raw }}<?= cms_richtext('field_name') ?>
{{ @global_name }}<?= cms_global('global_name') ?>
{% if field_name %}<?php if (cms_toggle('field_name')): ?>
{% for item in collection.post %}<?php cms_collection_list('post', function($item) { ?>
{% include 'nav' %}<?php include $__partials['nav']; ?>
{# comment #}(removed entirely)

The compiled PHP is written to a file in cache/templates/. The cache filename is an MD5 hash of the source template's real file path, so each template has a unique, deterministic cache key.

Automatic invalidation

The template cache is invalidated automatically based on file modification time. Before serving a cached template, Outpost compares the filemtime() of the source .html file against the filemtime() of the compiled cache file. If the source is newer, the template is recompiled and the cache is updated.

This means during development, editing and saving a .html template takes effect on the next page load with no manual steps required. The mtime check has negligible overhead — it is a single filesystem call per template.

Note — Partials have their own cache entries, separate from the page templates that include them. Modifying partials/nav.html invalidates only the nav partial's cache. Other cached templates remain valid. Only the pages that include the nav partial will recompile their partial cache on the next request — the top-level page template cache is not touched.

Cache file naming

Compiled templates are stored as PHP files in cache/templates/:

cache/templates/
  a1b2c3d4e5f6...php    ← compiled index.html
  7f8e9d0c1b2a...php    ← compiled about.html
  3c4d5e6f7a8b...php    ← compiled partials/nav.html
  9d0e1f2a3b4c...php    ← compiled partials/head.html

The filenames are MD5 hashes of the absolute file path of the source template. You will rarely need to inspect these files directly, but they are valid PHP and can be read if you need to debug a compilation issue.

HTML page cache

After a template is executed, its full HTML output is written to a file in cache/html/. On subsequent requests for the same URL within the TTL window, Outpost reads that file and sends it directly — executing no PHP template code and making no database queries.

Cache key

The HTML cache key is derived from the request URL and query string. Each unique URL gets its own cache entry:

/blog                    → cache/html/[hash-of-/blog].html
/blog?page=2             → cache/html/[hash-of-/blog?page=2].html
/post/hello-world        → cache/html/[hash-of-/post/hello-world].html

Paginated pages, filtered pages, and any URL with query parameters are cached separately from the base URL. This is the correct behavior — /blog and /blog?page=2 have different content.

TTL and expiry

The default TTL is 3600 seconds (1 hour). After an hour, the next request for that URL will re-execute the template, query the database for fresh content, and write a new cache file. The old file is replaced in-place.

This means newly published content will be visible on the site within 1 hour without any manual intervention. For most blogs and brochure sites, a 1-hour TTL is a good balance between freshness and performance.

Admin session bypass

When an admin session cookie is present, the HTML cache is bypassed entirely. Admins always see freshly rendered pages — this is essential when previewing content changes or testing template edits. Public visitors without the session cookie always hit the cache.

Cache bypass for development

During theme development, the template cache auto-invalidates when you save a file — but the HTML page cache does not, because it does not check whether the underlying template has changed since the HTML was generated.

Append ?nocache=1 to any URL to bypass the HTML cache for that request:

http://localhost:8000/blog?nocache=1
http://localhost:8000/post/hello-world?nocache=1

This forces a full re-render for that request without affecting the cache for other visitors. The cache entry is not updated when nocache=1 is present — it is a bypass, not an invalidation.

Tip — Logging into the admin panel sets the admin session cookie, which means your browser will bypass the HTML cache on all page views while you are logged in. You do not need to add ?nocache=1 manually if you are signed in to the admin.

Clearing the cache

There are three ways to clear the cache:

From the admin — Settings

Navigate to Admin → Settings and click Clear Cache. This deletes all files in both cache/templates/ and cache/html/. The next request for any page will recompile the template and rebuild the HTML cache from scratch.

Use this after deploying theme updates that affect many pages, or when you have edited theme files in a way that you want immediately reflected for all visitors (rather than waiting for the 1-hour TTL to expire).

From the admin — Content saves

When a content editor saves changes to a page or collection item in the admin, Outpost automatically invalidates the HTML cache entry for that specific page's URL. The item's cached HTML is deleted; the next visitor to that URL gets a freshly rendered page. Other pages are not affected.

Manual deletion

You can delete cache files directly on the server. Both cache directories contain only generated files — deleting any or all of them is safe. They will be regenerated automatically on the next request.

# Clear all HTML page cache
rm -f outpost/cache/html/*.html

# Clear all compiled templates
rm -f outpost/cache/templates/*.php

# Clear everything
rm -f outpost/cache/html/*.html outpost/cache/templates/*.php
Warning — Do not delete the cache/ directory itself, or the cache/html/ and cache/templates/ subdirectories. The directories must exist for Outpost to write new cache files. Only delete the files inside them.

Cache directory permissions

Both cache directories must be writable by the PHP process (the web server user). On a freshly deployed Outpost installation, set the cache directories to be writable:

chmod 755 outpost/cache/
chmod 755 outpost/cache/html/
chmod 755 outpost/cache/templates/

If the cache directories are not writable, Outpost will still serve pages — it simply will not be able to write cache files. Every request will re-execute the full template and database query. No errors are shown to visitors; the site degrades gracefully.

If you suspect caching is not working (pages seem slow, or fresh content is always shown immediately), check that the web server user has write access to outpost/cache/.

Development workflow

Understanding the two cache layers clarifies the right workflow when building and editing themes:

Editing template files (.html)

Save the file. The template cache invalidates automatically via the mtime check. The HTML page cache still has the old output. If you are logged into the admin, you will see the fresh output immediately (admin session bypasses HTML cache). If you are testing as a public visitor, either append ?nocache=1 or clear the cache from Settings.

Editing CSS and JS assets

Assets are served as static files — they bypass both cache layers entirely. Save the file and reload the browser. If the browser itself has cached the CSS, do a hard reload (Cmd+Shift+R or Ctrl+Shift+R). For persistent cache busting across all visitors, increment the version query string on the asset URL in your partial. See Assets for details.

Publishing new content

The admin save handler automatically purges the HTML cache for the affected page URL. Published changes are visible to the next visitor immediately. No manual cache clearing is needed after content edits.

Deploying theme updates to production

After uploading updated theme files to the server, the template cache will auto-invalidate via mtime on the next request for each page. If you want all pages to reflect the changes on the first request (rather than lazily on demand), clear the template cache from Admin → Settings after uploading.

Cache storage reference

DirectoryContentsDeleted by Clear Cache?Safe to delete manually?
cache/templates/ Compiled PHP from Liquid .html templates Yes Yes — regenerated automatically
cache/html/ Full rendered HTML output per URL Yes Yes — regenerated on next request
data/ SQLite database (cms.db) No No — contains all site content
uploads/ User-uploaded media files No No — contains uploaded content

Performance characteristics

With both cache layers functioning, Outpost's request path is as fast as a static file server for cached pages:

  1. PHP router runs (microseconds — path parsing only)
  2. HTML cache check — read one file from disk, send to browser
  3. Done. No template execution, no database queries.

On a cache miss (first request or after TTL expiry):

  1. PHP router runs
  2. HTML cache miss detected
  3. Template cache checked — compiled PHP loaded (or recompiled if source changed)
  4. Compiled template executes — database queries run, output generated
  5. HTML output written to cache/html/
  6. HTML output sent to browser

Cache misses are fast in absolute terms — SQLite is an embedded database with sub-millisecond query times for typical Outpost data volumes. But cache hits are faster still, and they do not hold a database connection, making the system efficient under concurrent load.
